structured_choices.data_source: Search logs from production e-commerce platform spanning 18 months (months 1–18). Includes user queries, product impressions (ranked order and position), clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, session identifiers, timestamps, user IDs (anonymized), and product catalog snapshots (title, description, category, price, inventory, sales velocity). Data is stratified into three non-overlapping temporal windows: training (months 1–12), validation (months 13–15), test (months 16–18). This ensures no temporal leakage and captures seasonal and catalog drift.
structured_choices.label_definition: Labels are derived from implicit feedback using a position-adjusted click signal. For each query-session, products are labeled as relevant (1) if clicked within 5 minutes of impression and the session resulted in add-to-cart or purchase within 30 minutes; products are labeled as non-relevant (0) if they received impression but no click, or clicked but the session did not convert. To reduce position bias, a secondary set of labels uses inverse propensity weighting (IPW): click probability is estimated from the baseline ranker's position distribution in the training set, and relevance labels are re-weighted by 1/P(click|position) to approximate unbiased relevance. Both labelings are used: implicit for primary offline metric, IPW-adjusted for diagnostic analysis of bias.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal (chronological) split on months 1–12 (train), 13–15 (validation), 16–18 (test). Justification: Search behavior is non-stationary due to seasonality, promotions, catalog evolution, and trending queries. Chronological split respects the temporal causality of the task and prevents the model from learning from future information; it also ensures the test set reflects realistic deployment conditions (model ranking on unseen query-product pairs and seasonal shifts). A random split would inflate performance estimates by allowing the model to interpolate across time periods with similar query-product pairs, masking generalization failure.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (text tokenizers, embedding models, scaling parameters for numerical features) are fit on the training set (months 1–12) only. Embeddings are generated using a pre-trained semantic model (e.g., a dense retrieval model fine-tuned on e-commerce data or a frozen transformer), not fit during the experiment; this is held constant. Product popularity and session statistics used as features are computed from the training window only and frozen before validation/test. This prevents data leakage and ensures the validation and test sets measure how the model generalizes to held-out time periods, not how well it memorizes training-set statistics.
structured_choices.features: Proposed model (learning-to-rank with semantic embeddings): (1) Query-product semantic similarity: cosine distance between query embedding and product title+description embedding (pre-trained dense model), (2) BM25 lexical score (query vs. product title), (3) Click-through history: fraction of sessions in training set where product was clicked given queries in same category, (4) Recent engagement: add-to-cart and purchase rates of the product in the most recent 4 weeks of training data, (5) Catalog features: product price (log-scaled), inventory level (log-scaled), category match to query (binary), sales velocity (log-scaled). Baseline (BM25 + handcrafted): (1) BM25 lexical score (query vs. product title), (2) Static popularity: product's click-through rate in training set, (3) Categorical relevance: category match, (4) Price and inventory features (same as proposed model). The baseline excludes semantic embeddings and temporal engagement signals to isolate the contribution of semantic similarity and recency.
structured_choices.model: Learning-to-rank model: LambdaMART or gradient boosted decision tree ranker (e.g., LightGBM Rank) trained on the training set to optimize NDCG@10. The model receives feature vectors for each (query, product) pair and learns a ranking function. Hyperparameters (number of trees, tree depth, learning rate, regularization) are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 13–15) using NDCG@10 as the selection metric. The baseline ranker is a simple learned-to-rank model using only the baseline feature set, with identical hyperparameter tuning effort and identical LambdaMART/LightGBM rank configuration.
structured_choices.baseline: A LambdaMART or LightGBM rank model trained on the same training set using only the baseline feature set (BM25, static popularity, categorical, price, inventory). Tuning approach: the baseline is given the same hyperparameter search budget (grid search, same search space, same validation metric NDCG@10 on months 13–15 data). This ensures the baseline is not handicapped by undertuning. The comparison isolates the value of semantic embeddings and recency signals; any NDCG gain is attributable to these features, not to stronger model optimization.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Offline NDCG@10 (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank position 10) computed on the test set (months 16–18). NDCG@10 is justified because: (1) It rewards ranking relevant items higher, directly aligning with the user intent and discovery goal, (2) Position discount (log2(rank+1)) reflects realistic user attention patterns (most users examine top 10 results), (3) Normalization makes it comparable across queries with different numbers of relevant items, (4) It is standard in learning-to-rank and allows comparison to literature. Computed as: for each query in the test set, NDCG@10 = (DCG@10) / (iDCG@10), where DCG sums (rel / log2(rank+1)) over ranked products, and iDCG is the ideal DCG if perfect ranking were achieved.
secondary_metrics: Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR@10): inverse position of the first relevant item, sensitive to ranking of the most relevant product.
Recall@10: fraction of relevant products retrieved in top 10, measures coverage of relevant inventory.
Click-through rate on test set: proportion of impressions that receive clicks, simulates real-world user engagement.
NDCG@10 computed on IPW-adjusted labels: diagnostic metric to assess robustness against position bias; if semantic model gains hold on IPW labels, the improvement is not confounded by exposure bias.
Per-category NDCG@10: stratified analysis to check whether gains are driven by branded/high-frequency queries or generalize across categories.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 13–15) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. NDCG@10 on the validation set is the selection metric. The model with the highest validation NDCG@10 is selected and evaluated once on the test set. No cross-validation is used; temporal structure makes k-fold inappropriate, and a single validation window is sufficient given the volume of data (3 months of search logs).
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 16–18) is held out during all model development and hyperparameter tuning. It is evaluated exactly once, at the end, after all model selection is complete. No model selection, threshold adjustment, or feature engineering is performed using the test set. The reported NDCG@10, MRR@10, and Recall@10 come from a single forward pass of the final model on held-out test data.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Position bias (exposure bias): Addressed via IPW-adjusted secondary labels and stratified analysis. If the semantic model outperforms the baseline on IPW-adjusted NDCG@10 as well, the gain is not a confound of position bias. Popularity priors: Baseline includes static popularity features to control for popularity effects; if semantic embeddings add value beyond popularity, the gain is not reducible to popularity. Branded query effects: Per-category NDCG@10 decomposition checks whether gains concentrate in branded or high-frequency queries (confound candidate) or generalize. Temporal drift: Temporal split (train–validation–test) is chronological, so the test set reflects future distribution shift; this is by design and part of realistic evaluation, not a confound. Catalog changes: Product features (inventory, price, sales velocity) are recomputed from training window and frozen; catalog drift is part of the generalization test. No held-out population stratification is required because the temporal split implicitly stratifies by time period, and per-category analysis provides additional stratification.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Learning-to-Rank with Semantic Embeddings for E-Commerce Search

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
This experiment tests whether a learning-to-rank model that incorporates query-to-product semantic embeddings and recent engagement signals outperforms a baseline ranker using lexical BM25 and static popularity features. The hypothesis predicts that semantic similarity will better capture user intent and recency signals will improve relevance prediction, resulting in higher NDCG@10 on held-out search sessions.

### 2. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 18 months of production search logs from the e-commerce platform. Raw data includes user queries, product impressions (with original rank position), clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, session identifiers, and timestamps. Product metadata (title, description, category, price, inventory, sales velocity) are included as catalog features. The temporal span (18 months) is chosen to capture seasonal variation and catalog drift, ensuring the test set reflects realistic non-stationary conditions.

Labels are defined using implicit feedback: a product is marked relevant (1) if it received an impression and the user clicked within 5 minutes, and the session converted to add-to-cart or purchase within 30 minutes. Non-relevant products (0) are those that received impression but no click, or were clicked without session conversion. This definition aligns with the business objective of helping users discover products that lead to engagement and purchase intent.

To diagnose position bias, a secondary label set is computed using inverse propensity weighting (IPW). Click probability at each position is estimated from the baseline ranker's position distribution in the training data, and labels are reweighted by 1/P(click|position). IPW labels will be used for sensitivity analysis to ensure gains are not confounded by exposure bias.

### 3. Data Splits and Temporal Causality
Data is divided chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, months 16–18 for testing. This temporal split respects the causal structure of the ranking task and prevents temporal leakage. A random split would be inappropriate because search behavior is non-stationary due to seasonality, catalog changes, and shifting query trends. By holding out the most recent 6 months for validation and test, the experiment measures how well the model generalizes to unseen query-product pairs and adapts to distribution shift—the actual deployment challenge.

### 4. Feature Engineering

**Proposed Model Features:**
- Query-product semantic similarity (cosine distance between pre-trained embeddings)
- BM25 lexical relevance score
- Product click-through history in training window (category-adjusted)
- Recent engagement: add-to-cart and purchase rates from most recent 4 weeks of training data
- Product metadata: log-scaled price, log-scaled inventory, category match, log-scaled sales velocity

**Baseline Features:**
- BM25 lexical score
- Static popularity (product click-through rate in training set)
- Category match
- Price and inventory (same as proposed model)

All text embeddings are generated using a pre-trained dense retrieval model (frozen, not fine-tuned in this experiment) to isolate the value of the semantic features from model training improvements. Preprocessing (tokenization, normalization, scaling) is fit on the training set only and applied uniformly to validation and test. This prevents data leakage and ensures reproducibility.

### 5. Model Configuration and Baseline
Both the proposed model and baseline use LambdaMART or LightGBM Rank, trained to optimize NDCG@10. Hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, regularization, number of iterations) are tuned via grid search using the validation set (months 13–15) with NDCG@10 as the selection metric. The baseline receives identical tuning effort and algorithm—it is handicapped only by the restricted feature set, ensuring that any performance difference is attributed to the semantic and recency signals, not to model selection imbalance.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Offline NDCG@10 on the test set. NDCG@10 directly measures ranking quality of relevant products within the typical user viewing window (top 10 results), aligning with the user discovery and conversion objectives. It rewards correct ranking of relevant items and discounts errors deeper in the list, reflecting user attention patterns.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR@10): Sensitivity to the position of the highest-ranked relevant item
- Recall@10: Coverage of relevant products in the top 10
- Click-through rate on the test set: Real-world proxy for engagement
- NDCG@10 on IPW-adjusted labels: Robustness against position bias
- Per-category NDCG@10: Stratified analysis to detect whether gains are concentrated in branded or high-frequency queries (potential confound) or generalize

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity
Model selection is performed on the validation set (months 13–15). The model configuration with the highest validation NDCG@10 is selected. The test set is held out during all hyperparameter tuning and feature engineering, and is evaluated exactly once at the end. No threshold adjustment, model retraining, or feature selection is performed using test data.

### 8. Confound Controls
**Position Bias:** Addressed via secondary IPW-adjusted labels and diagnostic analysis. If the semantic model outperforms the baseline on both standard and IPW labels, the gain is robust to exposure bias.

**Popularity Bias:** The baseline explicitly includes static popularity features. Any additional gain from the semantic model reflects semantic signal beyond popularity, not confounding with popularity priors.

**Branded Query Effects:** Per-category NDCG@10 analysis identifies whether gains concentrate in specific query types (e.g., branded high-volume searches) or generalize, revealing whether the improvement is driven by easy-to-rank queries or true semantic understanding.

**Temporal Drift:** The temporal split is designed to test generalization under distribution shift; drift is not a confound but an evaluation objective.

This design yields a methodologically sound comparison that isolates the contribution of semantic embeddings and recency signals while controlling for temporal dynamics, position bias, and popularity effects.
